agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is proposed that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two established production invariants in the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be delivered, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment. Once assigned, the Carrier-Shipment binding is locked."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does not exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"The existing invariant that once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"The proposed feature to automatically divide an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments with different carriers, bypassing supervisor approval."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. If a Shipment has zero or multiple Carriers, accountability for delivery is undefined and the system is in a corrupt state. Every downstream process (tracking, billing, liability, proof-of-delivery) depends on a single Carrier being responsible.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-terminal state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. It prevents unauthorized mid-flight carrier changes that could cause lost shipments, billing disputes, and chain-of-custody violations. Removing this guard corrupts the trust model.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change a Shipment's Carrier without Supervisor authorization must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a physical constraint — a shipment cannot leave a facility without a single accountable carrier. Dispatch without this guarantee breaks chain-of-custody.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A 501 kg shipment is auto-split into two sub-shipments. The original Carrier was already assigned. Who owns the parent? Who owns the children? What happens to the original assignment lock?","expected_behavior":"Undefined under current model — this is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment's carrier fails to pick up. Under current rules, only a Supervisor can reassign. But auto-split was designed to bypass supervisors.","expected_behavior":"Contradiction: the feature removes supervisor oversight for initial assignment, but the invariant requires it for reassignment. Failure recovery is undefined."},{"scenario":"A shipment of 1500 kg is split into 3 sub-shipments. One is damaged in transit. Liability and insurance claims require a single accountable carrier per shipment.","expected_behavior":"If the parent shipment no longer has a single carrier, liability attribution is ambiguous — a real business and legal risk."},{"scenario":"Customer tracking: the customer booked one shipment but now there are three sub-shipments with three carriers and potentially three different ETAs.","expected_behavior":"Customer experience degrades. The customer-facing 'shipment' concept fractures without a clear aggregation model."},{"scenario":"Billing: the original shipment had a negotiated rate with one carrier. Auto-split creates assignments to carriers who may have different rate agreements.","expected_behavior":"Financial reconciliation breaks. The original contract/rate may not apply to the sub-shipments."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments across carriers changes rate structures, may void negotiated contracts, and creates billing reconciliation complexity. Liability in damage/loss scenarios becomes ambiguous across multiple carriers.","operational":"Major disruption. Every downstream system — tracking, dispatch, proof-of-delivery, returns, customer support — assumes one carrier per shipment. Introducing multi-carrier shipments without redesigning these systems will cause failures.","user":"Customers lose single-shipment visibility. Instead of tracking one delivery, they now track multiple sub-deliveries with different ETAs and carriers. Support complexity increases.","risk":"Critical. Two production invariants are violated simultaneously. This is not a policy change — it is a structural contradiction with the existing domain model. Shipping without resolving this will corrupt system state.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Dispatch workflows assume one carrier per shipment. Auto-split breaks this assumption and requires new processes."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is bypassed, undermining the control model that exists for good reason (accountability, liability)."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Carriers are assigned to sub-shipments without the normal locked-assignment guarantee. Their operational planning may be disrupted."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Single-shipment tracking and accountability is fractured. Customer experience degrades for heavy shipments."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Multi-carrier shipments require new billing logic, rate negotiation per sub-shipment, and reconciliation processes that do not exist today."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody, liability, and insurance all assume single-carrier accountability per shipment. Splitting without legal review creates exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between the proposed feature and two established production invariants. The feature as stated cannot be implemented without violating BR-1 (one carrier per shipment) and BR-2 (supervisor-only reassignment). Additionally, the concept of 'sub-shipment' introduces a new aggregate that has legal and compliance implications requiring review. Confidence is below 50 because the contradictions are unresolvable without domain model changes that the spec does not acknowledge.","high_confidence_areas":["The two invariants (single carrier, supervisor lock) are clearly stated and in production","The proposed feature directly contradicts both invariants","The edge cases around liability, billing, and tracking are real business risks"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments or a new entity type","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard physical constraint or a business policy","Whether there is an existing legal framework for multi-carrier liability splitting"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants are correctly stated and enforced in production","Sub-shipment is a new concept not currently in the domain model","The product team has not yet consulted legal/compliance on multi-carrier liability"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple carriers to what originates as a single shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one carrier per shipment). Even if sub-shipments are introduced as new entities, the parent shipment's carrier assignment is disrupted without supervisor involvement, violating BR-2.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Supervisors","Customers","Finance","Legal"],"options":["A) Reject the feature as incompatible with the current domain model.","B) Introduce a 'Composite Shipment' aggregate that contains sub-shipments, each preserving the one-carrier invariant, but require Supervisor approval for the split (preserving BR-2).","C) Reclassify auto-split as a Supervisor-level action, making it a system-suggested split that a Supervisor must approve before carrier assignment proceeds.","D) Evolve the domain model to support multi-carrier shipments as a new bounded context, with full redesign of tracking, billing, liability, and dispatch."],"recommendation":"Option C in the short term: the system suggests an optimal split for overweight shipments, but a Supervisor must approve. This preserves both invariants while delivering the business value of automated split calculation. Option B is the medium-term architectural path if the business wants to formalize composite shipments."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The 500 kg threshold origin is unclear. Is this a physical constraint (vehicle capacity), a regulatory limit, a carrier contract term, or an arbitrary business policy? The answer determines whether the threshold is an invariant or a configurable policy.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Carriers","Legal"],"options":["Clarify with product team whether 500 kg is a hard physical/regulatory constraint or a soft business policy."],"recommendation":"Determine the source of the 500 kg limit before encoding it as a business rule."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and compliance implications of multi-carrier liability for a single customer order have not been reviewed. Chain-of-custody, insurance, and damage liability models assume single-carrier accountability.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Customers","Finance"],"options":["Engage legal team to review multi-carrier liability framework before proceeding."],"recommendation":"Block feature implementation until legal review is complete."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment origin without supervisor involvement","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier. BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier."},{"violation":"The feature introduces the concept of 'sub-shipment' which does not exist in the current domain model and has undefined semantics for tracking, billing, liability, and dispatch","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-3: All downstream systems assume one carrier per shipment at dispatch. Sub-shipments break this assumption without a corresponding domain model evolution."}]}
